figures.caption: Figure 3: Evaluation suite. ForesightFlow is evaluated in both simulation and the real world: (a) Turning on radio, (b) Picking up trash, (c) Spraying fruit trees, (d) Cook hot dogs, (e) Wash a baseball cap, (f) Set-Paper-Roll, (g) Pick-Trash, (h) Cube-Stack, (i) Transfer-Food, and (j) Wipe-Whiteboard. The tasks require long-horizon sequencing, bimanual coordination, contact-rich manipulation, and precision placement.
Figure 10: Hardware overview of the TeleAvatar robot and its teleoperation system.
Figure 14: Cook hot dogs
Figure 13: Spraying fruit trees
Table 8: Hyperparameters of ForesightFlow on real-world robot (based on π0).
Table 9: Hyperparameters of BC on simulation (based on π0.5).

Figure 1: ForesightFlow as a self-guided flow policy. ForesightFlow augments each flow endpoint with action and success-potential coordinates. Decoupled training applies advantage weights only to action velocities while uniformly supervising potential velocities for calibration. At inference, generated potentials rank candidate action chunks without a separate critic.
Figure 19: Transfer-Food
Table 10: Hyperparameters of BC on real-world robot (based on π0).
Figure 9: The architecture of the value model in Implicit Diffusion Q-Learning (IDQL).
Figure 25: Success and failure trajectory frames for the simulated Spraying fruit trees task.
Figure 23: Success and failure trajectory frames for the simulated Turning on radio task.
Figure 28: Success and failure trajectory frames for the real-world Wipe-Whiteboard task.
Figure 27: Success and failure trajectory frames for the real-world Cube-Stack task.

Figure 6: NFE=1 vs NFE=100 score scatter (per task). Each point is a candidate action’s progress score under the two NFE settings. Monotone alignment along the diagonal indicates ranking preservation across all five tasks.
Figure 7: Per-task ranking fidelity. (a) Per-task Kendall τ box plot. (b) τ remains high on both success and failure trajectories, addressing concerns about failure-mode error distribution.
Table 11: Hyperparameters of value network of IDQL.

Figure 4: Stage-wise real-world progress. Normalized completion score for each stage across five bimanual tasks. ForesightFlow improves most on stages where failures are recoverable and partial trajectories provide useful supervision.

Table 5: Cross-task NFE=1 vs NFE=100 ranking fidelity. For each held-out observation, K = 8 candidates are scored with both estimators. The one-step estimator largely preserves the candidate ranking used by self-guided inference. The MC gain measures the fraction of the NFE=100 best-of-K improvement retained by NFE=1 ranking, and κ reports trajectory curvature in the success-potential subspace.

Figure 2: Decoupled AWR in ForesightFlow. A stopgradient boundary pass at σ = 0 produces a one-step potential baseline and an advantage weight. A randomtime CFM update then applies this weight only to the action velocity, while the potential velocity remains uniformly supervised on both successes and failures.
Figure 29: Success and failure trajectory frames for the real-world Transfer-Food task.

Figure 8: Trajectory geometry and value calibration. (a) Curvature κ in the success-potential subspace is concentrated near zero (median ≤ 0.039 across tasks), supporting the near-linearity assumption. (b) Per-stage value violin plot shows separation between success and failure trajectories.
Figure 22: Success and failure trajectory frames for the simulated Cook Hot Dogs task.
Figure 15: Wash a baseball cap

Figure 21: Dataset composition. Frame-level distribution of expert and autonomous rollout data across the five simulation and five real-world tasks, including successful and failed trajectories.
